text: The surgical team positions a 12mm and a 5mm trocar along the umbilicus line, with the optical device placed between the xiphoid process and the umbilicus, preparing to manage a large 15cm myoma.

The surgeon attempts to control both uterine arteries at their origin by opening the pericardium lateral to the infundibulopelvic ligament, allowing the insufflated gas to assist in the dissection. The large volume of the myoma presents a challenge in identifying the optimal dissecting plane.

The surgeon identifies the umbilical artery and ureter, using traction and counter traction to develop the pararectal fossa. The surgical field is expanded to reveal the anatomical structures, preparing to develop the paradigmal fossa.

The pararectal fossa and paramedical fossa are clearly visible. The uterine artery is seen running medially to the umbilical artery. The surgeon prepares to safely place a clip on the uterine artery after medializing the left ureter.

The surgeon uses a standard clip to secure the uterine artery on one side and proceeds to replicate the procedure on the opposite side. The right infundibulopelvic ligament is tracked medially, and the peritoneum is opened laterally to it, while the assistant works to expand the retroperitoneal space.

The surgeon uses traction and counter traction to push the large myoma to the left, facilitating the development of the pararectal fossa. The ureter is visible above the surgical device. The internal iliac artery, umbilical artery, and two uterine arteries are clearly seen, with a clip applied to control the uterine arteries and decrease vascularization.

The surgeon develops both pararectal and paramedical fossae to place a clip perpendicularly on the uterine arteries, effectively controlling both with a single clip. The uterus is now well devascularized. A longitudinal incision is made medially using bipolar scissors.

The surgeon opens the uterine serosa progressively and coagulates the myometrial capsule until reaching the large myoma. The procedure appears bloodless, revealing the big myoma clearly. The hystereotomy is increased to accommodate the myoma's volume, with a typical incision size of ten centimeters for a fifteen-centimeter myoma.

The surgeon uses laparoscopic 10mm forceps placed laterally to the myoma to track and attempt extraction of the myoma from its bed. Assistants track the healthy myoma trio while the surgeon applies bipolar energy to the myoma when encountering large vessels and veins, avoiding the healthy tissue.

The surgeon identifies a good dissecting plane, making the myomectomy procedure visually simple and bloodless. The surgeon coagulates a large vein using bipolar energy and continues lateral traction on the myoma, while assistants track the myometrium on the opposite side. The myomectomy is nearly complete, achieved primarily through traction without using energy inside the myoma bed to prevent endometrial burning.

The surgeon removes the entire myoma from the uterus and cleans the myoma bed, removing blood from the rectovaginal space. Using a 14mm needle with Vicryl suture, stitches are placed laterally and far from the scar edge to invaginate healthy myometrium into the myoma bed. This technique ensures perfect coaptation, reducing the risk of intramyometrial hematoma and postoperative bleeding, while creating a strong scar. Assistants help invaginate the myometrium, and intracorporeal knotting is used for precise stitch control.

The surgeon observes good ischemia at the edge of the scar and proceeds to place three or four stitches using the intracorporeal knotting technique. The stitches are positioned four centimeters laterally on both the left and right sides to invaginate the healthy myometrium into the myoma bed, aiming for effective hemostasis and reducing the risk of intramyometrial hematoma.

The surgeon places five or six stitches for a 15-centimeter diameter myoma, ensuring perfect myometrial coaptation and cicatrization. The stitches are applied with good invagination, using a double flat knot technique for quick suturing to minimize bleeding during the procedure.

The surgeon places the final knot on the posterior part of the uterine fundus using a 14mm needle, ensuring the stitch does not involve the rectum. The double flat knot and intracorporeal knotting technique are employed to achieve a hemostatic stitch and strong scar formation.

The surgeon places a large endoscopic bag with a capacity of 1200 cc to contain the entire myoma. The myoma is placed inside the bag, and morcellation begins within the bag using a morcellator set at 700 rods. Care is taken to avoid including any bowel in the morcellator. After completing the morcellation, the surgeon removes the bag and cleans the pelvic area. The incision used for the morcellator is closed to reduce the risk of incisional hernia.
